did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin lesion classification (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and non-cancerous lesions) using datasets from ISIC and DermNet. The model was deployed on a Raspberry Pi 5 for real-time detection and evaluation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and deployment of a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 CNN on a Raspberry Pi 5 for skin cancer detection. It reports new empirical results, including specific accuracy (96.71%), precision, recall, and F1-scores, along with statistical analysis (Pearson’s correlation, t-test) and a confusion matrix, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An AI-based Skin Cancer Detection Using Raspberry Pi
journal: Proc. of International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA 2025)
brief_summary: This study developed an AI-powered skin cancer detection system using a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network integrated into a Raspberry Pi 5 microcontroller for real-time classification. The model was trained on an augmented dataset comprising images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and DermNet, classifying melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and non-cancerous lesions. The system achieved an average accuracy of 96.71%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 88.62%, 92.48%, and 90.15%, respectively. Evaluation involved 80 trials on unseen images, revealing challenges in distinguishing squamous cell carcinoma due to visual similarity and dataset underrepresentation. The results demonstrate that the lightweight architecture and optimized deep learning approach provide an efficient, low-cost, and portable screening solution suitable for resource-limited settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary skin cancer types classified by the model. Included in the dataset with 7,319 images for fine-tuning. The model achieved 90% accuracy (18/20) in device testing.

One of the three primary skin cancer types classified by the model. Included in the dataset with 4,921 images for fine-tuning. The model achieved 90% accuracy (18/20) in device testing. Noted to have significant confusion with melanoma.

One of the three primary skin cancer types classified by the model. Included in the dataset with 1,472 images for fine-tuning. The model achieved 80% accuracy (16/20) in device testing. Identified as underrepresented in the dataset, leading to classification challenges.
The benign comparator class in the four-class classification task. Included in the dataset with 11,636 images for fine-tuning. The model achieved 95% accuracy (19/20) in device testing.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Non-Cancerous Lesions.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a four-class classification task using a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 CNN with a softmax output layer. The model predicts discrete lesion classes (Melanoma, BCC, SCC, Non-Cancerous) based on dermoscopic images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with a confusion matrix showing true/false positives/negatives for each class.

datasets_used.name: Combined ISIC and DermNet Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and DermNet, consisting of dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The dataset was augmented (rotation, flipping, zooming) and preprocessed (resized to 224x224, normalized).

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the MobileNetV2 CNN model for 12 epochs with early stopping.
use.validation: Used to monitor validation accuracy and loss during training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification performance via confusion matrices and metrics (precision, recall, F1-score).

datasets_used.name: Device Testing Set (Unseen Images)
datasets_used.description: A set of 80 unseen images (20 per class) not included in the training, validation, or testing datasets, used for real-time evaluation on the Raspberry Pi 5 prototype.

use.testing: Used for 80 trials of real-time classification on the Raspberry Pi 5 device to assess practical performance and accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the relationship and covariance between the number of training epochs and training accuracy, as well as between epochs and validation accuracy.
To determine the statistical significance of the difference between mean training accuracy and mean validation accuracy across 12 epochs.
Used as the cutoff for the paired t-test to determine whether to reject the null hypothesis regarding the difference in mean accuracies.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference between the mean training accuracy and the mean validation accuracy across the 12 epochs.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference between the mean training accuracy and the mean validation accuracy across the 12 epochs.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The p-value (0.0608) was greater than the alpha threshold of 0.05, leading to the failure to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates that there is no statistically significant difference between the mean training accuracy and the mean validation accuracy.

Combined ISIC and DermNet Dataset (Test.notes: The model was trained for 12 epochs. Training accuracy reached 98.65% at epoch 12, while validation accuracy was 91.61%. A paired t-test showed no statistically significant difference between mean training accuracy (95.12%) and mean validation accuracy (90.73%) (p=0.0608). The model struggled to distinguish squamous cell carcinoma due to dataset underrepresentation and visual similarity with basal cell carcinoma and melanoma.

Device Testing Set (Unseen Images on Ras.notes: Misclassifications predominantly occurred between squamous cell carcinoma and other lesion types. Lighting conditions and inconsistent image focus contributed to confusion. The dataset was not balanced, particularly underrepresenting squamous cell carcinoma.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights (implied by 'fine-tuned' and standard MobileNetV2 usage, though specific source dataset like ImageNet is not explicitly named in the provided text, it is a standard backbone). The paper states 'Initial layers were frozen to preserve general image features'.

architecture_details.modification: Fine-tuned. Initial layers were frozen. Deeper layers were retrained. Layers were selectively unfrozen to enhance feature learning.

training_details.regularization: Early stopping was implemented to mitigate overfitting.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation, flipping, and zooming
Use of Neopixel WS2812B light for consistent illumination to reduce shadows and reflections

conclusions: The study successfully developed an AI-powered skin cancer detection system using a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network integrated into a Raspberry Pi 5 microcontroller.

The system achieved an average accuracy of 96.71%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 88.62%, 92.48%, and 90.15%, respectively, demonstrating superior classification performance compared to prior studies using similar hardware.

The model exhibited high accuracy in classifying non-cancerous lesions (95% on device testing) and melanoma (90%) and basal cell carcinoma (90%), but struggled to distinguish squamous cell carcinoma (80% accuracy) due to visual similarity with other classes and underrepresentation in the dataset.

Challenges in distinguishing squamous cell carcinoma were attributed to dataset imbalance, visual similarity in lesion texture, and variations in lighting conditions and image focus during device testing.
The developed system offers an efficient, low-cost, and portable preliminary screening solution suitable for resource-limited settings where access to specialized dermatologists is limited.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the lack of evaluation in real-world clinical settings and across demographically diverse populations, highlighting the need for future clinical trials, cross-dataset validation, and fairness audits.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation in real-world clinical settings or across demographically diverse populations, limiting generalizability.

The dataset is imbalanced, with Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) significantly underrepresented (1,472 images vs. 11,636 non-cancerous), leading to lower accuracy (80%) for SCC compared to other classes.
The model exhibits significant confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma and Melanoma, as well as between SCC and other classes, due to visual similarity in lesion texture.
Device testing relied on a small, balanced set of 80 unseen images (20 per class), which may not reflect the true performance distribution given the underlying dataset imbalance.
Performance on the device was affected by practical factors such as lighting conditions and inconsistent image focus, which were not controlled in the training data.
The paper acknowledges potential bias in AI models regarding varying skin tones but does not report specific fairness audits or demographic breakdowns of the ISIC/DermNet data used.

Statistical significance testing (paired t-test) showed no significant difference between training and validation accuracy (p=0.0608), but this does not guarantee robustness against overfitting on unseen clinical data.
The system is a prototype; long-term reliability, user interface usability, and integration into clinical workflows were not evaluated.
